text.title: Micro-MVP Experiment Types
text.summary: Six micro-MVP experiment types mapped to effort range and what each one validates.

rule.statement: Match the experiment type to the assumption category. Do not build a landing page to validate willingness-to-pay; use a smoke test instead.
rule.rationale: Each experiment type has a different signal type. Using the wrong type wastes time and produces misleading data.

list.item: Landing page (1 day): validates demand and messaging. Shows a description of the product and measures sign-up rate or email capture.
Concierge (1 week): validates workflow and core value. Manually deliver the product's promise to 5–10 users to discover the real job-to-be-done.
Wizard of Oz (days): validates UX and willingness-to-pay. Fake automated product backed by manual human fulfilment; users interact as if it is real.
Video demo (hours): validates interest and virality. Record the product promise; measure views, shares, and comments before building anything.
Fake door (hours): validates feature demand. Add a button or menu item for a feature that does not exist yet; measure click rate before building.
Smoke test (days): validates price sensitivity and conversion. Offer the product for purchase; measure conversion at the target price point before delivering.

example.note: Assumption: people want easy file sync across devices.
Micro-MVP: a 3-minute video explaining the concept, posted online. No code written.
Result: waitlist jumped from 5,000 to 75,000 overnight. Validated demand without building a single byte of sync infrastructure.
